title: Support of unknown values (X) in design
body:
Hi !

First of all, thanks _a lot_ for the amazing tool that is Verilator.

### TL; DR
This request is about `X` support, propagation and dump in waves, maybe status about this feature or just some information about a very blocking point.

### More details

While I know that Verilator is mainly a two-state simulator, I saw that a partial support for High-impedance (`Z`) value is available.
This request would be about the `X` value.

Today, `X` values are "supported" through resolution to 1, 0 or random value, depending on the `--x-assign` parameter.
While this works well, it also can hide issues (mainly initialization issues) that pop up later in the design stage (linting, synthesis...) and are not detected with Verilator.

The X propagation is also used in verification (imagine an ADC or a memory that outputs `X` when its data is not available) and could also be used in some very corner cases for testing the robustness of designs through X injection. It would, in fact, be the only remaining feature missing from Verilator with regards to verification after the implementation of timing support and the support of UVM.

I am not very familiar with Verilator flow itself as I use it mostly through cocotb, so I might be mistaken on a lot of my assumptions. However I suppose that, as the `X` value is _resolved_, it is indeed detected and _could_ be kept as-is and propagated as appropriate through the logic ( I'm not naive enough to think that this is an easy job, though) and, finally dumped in the waveforms. 
Also, this would probably lead to a cost in performances.
To tackle this, it might be interesting to think about having a parameter that enable the support of `X` values in the verilated model altogether.

If there is a blocking factor (aside than "nobody wants to work on it", which is perfectly understandable) that an average user might not know about for the development of this feature, it might be worth mentioning it in the FAQ (or categorize this issue as Q&A).

Thanks again for Verilator and best regards !
